docs.title: Home
docs.text:
Emerging training platform

Missionary Readiness Institute

Practical training for faithful cross-cultural service.

Missionary Readiness Institute is developing a lightweight course platform for independent missionaries, mission-minded families, local churches, sponsors, and small organizations preparing for responsible cross-cultural service.

Explore courses About the platform

docs.title: What This Platform Is
docs.text:
This is a static, early-stage training site built to support better readiness conversations. It is not an accredited school, seminary, mission board, sending agency, full LMS, or finished certification ecosystem.

The goal is simple: provide clear course materials that help learners prepare with biblical faithfulness, cross-cultural humility, accountability, safety, and practical care.

docs.title: Start Here
docs.text:
Missionary Readiness 101 Beginner 45-60 min Available draft

Begin with the core readiness framework for learners, churches, sponsors, and families.

Course Catalog Foundational In development

Browse the first readiness tracks in formation, culture, care, language learning, and field entry.

Sponsor Brief For leaders One-page brief

Share the project problem, proposed solution, next steps, and ways supporters can help.

docs.title: Who It Serves
docs.text:
Missionaries and families

Introductory preparation for people discerning or preparing for cross-cultural service.

Churches and mentors

A shared framework for asking better readiness questions before sending or supporting workers.

Sponsors and partners

Practical language for responsible support, care, communication, and accountability.

docs.title: Training Approach
docs.text: Formation before activity. Readiness begins with Scripture, prayer, character, worship, and obedience. Humility before strategy. Cross-cultural service should honor local churches, local leaders, and local communities. Care before crisis. Preparation includes family safety, child protection, support systems, and appropriate boundaries. Practical next steps. Courses include lessons, reflection questions, knowledge checks, and assignments for discussion.

docs.title: Connect
docs.text:
Questions, corrections, partnership conversations, and course feedback may be sent to robert@tetra6.com.

Current status

This public site is an emerging readiness platform. Course materials are introductory and should be reviewed by responsible ministry leaders before use in formal training.

docs.title: About
docs.text:
Missionary Readiness Institute is an emerging online training platform focused on missionary readiness. It exists to help independent missionaries, church members, mission-minded families, local churches, and sponsoring partners prepare for cross-cultural service with humility, accountability, and practical care.

The platform is not a university, seminary, accredited institution, or sending authority. It is a practical training resource designed to support thoughtful preparation, local church involvement, and responsible mission practice.

docs.title: Why This Platform Exists
docs.text:
Missionary service often begins with sincere desire, but desire alone is not enough. Workers and supporters need space to think carefully about spiritual formation, cultural humility, safety, accountability, family realities, local church relationships, and practical preparation.

This site is being developed to provide that kind of starting point: clear public resources, course outlines, reflection questions, and practical assignments that can support better conversations before service begins.

docs.title: Who This Is For
docs.text:
• Independent missionaries preparing for service.
  • Church members discerning missionary involvement.
  • Mission-minded families considering cross-cultural work.
  • Local churches training or sending workers.
  • Sponsors and ministry partners who want to support workers responsibly.
  • Small organizations that need clear readiness guidance.

docs.title: What We Emphasize
docs.text:
Readiness is more than enthusiasm. It includes biblical faithfulness, spiritual maturity, cross-cultural respect, family and child safety, legal compliance, accountability, teachability, and practical service.

Courses and public pages are written to help learners ask better questions, prepare wisely, and serve in ways that honor local churches, local leaders, and the communities they hope to bless. The goal is not to create unnecessary barriers, but to encourage sober preparation and faithful service.

docs.title: Current Status
docs.text:
This site is still developing. Content should be treated as introductory training material, not as official accreditation, legal advice, professional counsel, or a replacement for church leadership, pastoral care, qualified professional guidance, or field-specific requirements.

As the platform matures, course pages may be expanded with additional lessons, facilitator notes, and certificates of completion. A certificate of completion would only indicate participation in course material; it would not be an accredited certification or approval for missionary service.

docs.title: Contact
docs.text: Use this page for questions about the public site, course content, partnerships, corrections, or responsible use of the training material.

docs.title: General Inquiries
docs.text: Email: robert@tetra6.com

docs.title: Content Feedback
docs.text:
When reporting a content issue, please include:

  • The page title or URL.
  • A short description of the concern.
  • Any suggested correction or source that would help reviewers.

docs.title: Ministry Partnerships
docs.text: Local churches, sponsors, small organizations, and training partners may use this contact page to start a conversation about adapting readiness material for their context.

docs.title: Safety And Sensitive Situations
docs.text:
This public contact channel is not designed for urgent care, confidential reporting, legal questions, immigration matters, medical concerns, child protection reports, or field security issues. Use the appropriate local authority, qualified professional, church leader, or organization-specific reporting process.

Note

Do not send confidential pastoral counseling, medical, legal, immigration, child protection, or security-sensitive details through this public contact channel.

docs.title: Mission & Vision
docs.text: Missionary Readiness Institute is an emerging training platform for people preparing to serve, send, sponsor, or support cross-cultural mission work. This page summarizes the purpose and commitments shaping the platform.

docs.title: Mission
docs.text: Missionary Readiness Institute provides practical, biblically faithful, and culturally respectful training that helps missionaries, churches, families, and sponsors prepare for responsible cross-cultural service.

docs.title: Vision
docs.text:
We want mission-minded people to enter service with humility, preparation, accountability, and love for the communities they serve. Readiness should strengthen local churches, protect vulnerable people, respect local leaders, and help workers serve with wisdom rather than impulse.

The vision is not to replace churches, mission boards, sending agencies, pastoral counsel, or field-specific preparation. The platform exists to support better readiness conversations and provide practical training resources that can be used by individuals, families, churches, sponsors, and small organizations.

docs.title: Audience
docs.text:
The platform is being developed for:

  • Independent missionaries preparing for service.
  • Church members discerning missionary involvement.
  • Mission-minded families preparing together.
  • Local churches training or sending workers.
  • Sponsors and ministry partners supporting responsibly.
  • Small organizations that need practical readiness guidance.

docs.title: Guiding Commitments
docs.text:
• Biblical faithfulness: Readiness begins with Scripture, prayer, worship, obedience, and character.
  • Cross-cultural humility: Workers enter new contexts as learners, not experts.
  • Local respect: Mission practice should honor local churches, local leaders, and local communities.
  • Safety and care: Preparation should consider family safety, child protection, personal health, and appropriate boundaries.
  • Accountability: Workers should remain connected to responsible churches, mentors, sponsors, and ministry leaders.
  • Practical service: Training should prepare learners for real decisions, field conditions, and ordinary acts of faithful service.

docs.title: What Success Looks Like
docs.text:
Learners should be able to name their next preparation steps, identify support needs and risks, communicate clearly with churches and sponsors, and approach cross-cultural ministry with sober hope and teachable confidence.

Churches and sponsors should be able to use the material to ask better questions, clarify expectations, identify concerns early, and support workers more responsibly.

docs.title: Current Status
docs.text:
This is an emerging training platform. It is not an accredited school, seminary, mission board, sending agency, or degree-granting institution.

The platform may eventually support certificates of completion for course participation. A certificate of completion would not be an accredited certification, academic degree, ordination, commissioning, or approval for missionary service.

docs.title: Sponsor Brief
docs.text:
Missionary Readiness Institute is an early-stage online training platform focused on practical missionary readiness. It is being developed to help independent missionaries, church members, mission-minded families, local churches, sponsors, and small organizations prepare for cross-cultural service with humility, accountability, biblical faithfulness, and practical care.

The platform is not a university, seminary, accredited institution, mission board, or sending agency. It is a service-oriented training resource meant to strengthen thoughtful preparation and better conversations before, during, and after missionary service.

docs.title: The Problem
docs.text:
Many mission-minded people step toward service with sincere desire but limited preparation. Independent missionaries and small sending networks may not have a shared framework for discussing spiritual formation, cross-cultural humility, safety, accountability, family realities, legal compliance, language learning, or long-term care.

Churches and sponsors often want to help but may lack practical tools for asking good questions, identifying risks early, and supporting workers responsibly.

docs.title: The Proposed Solution
docs.text:
The Missionary Readiness Platform provides a clear, public, beginner-friendly training library that can support readiness conversations between workers, churches, sponsors, families, mentors, and ministry leaders.

A minimum viable platform would include:

  • Public pages explaining the purpose, audience, and guiding commitments.
  • Starter policy pages for privacy, terms, acceptable use, copyright, and disclaimers.
  • A course catalog with foundational readiness topics.
  • A reusable course template for consistent course development.
  • Introductory courses with lessons, reflection questions, knowledge checks, practical assignments, facilitator notes, and completion criteria.
  • Clear language about certificates of completion without claiming accreditation.

docs.title: Who It Serves
docs.text:
• Independent missionaries: Practical preparation before serving alone or outside a large agency.
  • Local churches: A framework for discernment, mentoring, sending conversations, and care.
  • Sponsors and private donors: Better questions for responsible support and accountability.
  • Mission-minded families: Shared language for preparation, safety, communication, and expectations.
  • Small organizations: A lightweight training structure that can be reviewed, adapted, and improved.

docs.title: Why This Matters
docs.text:
Good intentions do not remove the need for preparation. Missionary readiness helps workers serve with greater humility, respect local churches and leaders, protect vulnerable people, communicate clearly with supporters, and recognize when qualified professional guidance is needed.

The goal is not to create unnecessary barriers. The goal is sober preparation for faithful service.

docs.title: Immediate Next Steps
docs.text:
The current priority is to develop a credible minimum viable platform:

  1. Polish the public website and policy pages.
  2. Expand the first course, Missionary Readiness 101.
  3. Build out foundational courses in spiritual formation, cross-cultural service, missionary care, language learning, and field readiness.
  4. Invite pastors, missionaries, sponsors, educators, and ministry leaders to review the material.
  5. Identify a simple process for feedback, revisions, and future certificates of completion.

docs.title: Ways Supporters Can Help
docs.text:
Supporters can help in practical, non-pressured ways:

  • Pray for wisdom, humility, clarity, and protection for the work.
  • Review course content for biblical faithfulness, cultural respect, and practical usefulness.
  • Introduce pastors, missionaries, sponsors, educators, or ministry leaders who could offer feedback.
  • Provide technical help with MkDocs, GitHub Pages, accessibility, editing, or workflow improvements.
  • Test courses and suggest clearer questions, assignments, or facilitator notes.
  • Help identify legal, safety, child protection, privacy, or compliance issues that need qualified review.
  • Provide financial support if appropriate and if a responsible receiving process is established.

docs.title: Current Status
docs.text: This is an early-stage but serious effort. The public site, policy framework, course template, and first course structure are being built now. The project should be evaluated as an emerging educational resource, not as a finished training ecosystem or accredited program.

docs.title: Course Catalog
docs.text:
The course catalog is a developing training library for missionary readiness. It is designed for independent missionaries, church members, mission-minded families, local churches, and sponsoring partners who need practical preparation before or alongside field-specific training.

The courses are introductory. They do not replace local church discernment, organizational requirements, professional advice, or supervised field preparation.

docs.title: Available And Developing Courses
docs.text:
Missionary Readiness 101 Beginner45-60 minAvailable draft

The basic readiness framework for new learners, churches, sponsors, and families.

Spiritual Formation Beginner60-75 minAvailable draft

Character, prayer, Scripture, worship, rest, obedience, and accountability.

Cross-Cultural Service Beginner60-75 minAvailable draft

Humility, listening, cultural learning, local leadership, and respectful service.

Missionary Care Beginner60-75 minAvailable draft

Accountability, support, family health, boundaries, care plans, and sustainability.

Language Learning Beginner45-60 minAvailable draft

Realistic expectations, listening-first practice, local helpers, and perseverance.

Field Readiness Beginner60-75 minAvailable draft

Practical field entry, logistics, communication, risk awareness, and first-year priorities.

docs.title: Course Pattern
docs.text:
Each course uses a consistent lightweight structure:

  • Overview and purpose.
  • Learning objectives.
  • Short lessons or readings.
  • Reflection questions.
  • Knowledge checks.
  • Practical assignments.
  • Facilitator notes and completion criteria.

docs.title: Recommended Sequence
docs.text:
1. Missionary Readiness 101
  2. Spiritual Formation
  3. Cross-Cultural Service
  4. Missionary Care
  5. Language Learning
  6. Field Readiness

This sequence starts with formation and posture before moving into practices, care, and field-entry decisions.

No accreditation claim

These courses are not academic degrees or accredited certifications. Any future credential language should use "certificate of completion" unless formal accreditation is actually obtained.

docs.title: Cross-Cultural Service
docs.text:
Cross-Cultural Service helps learners enter new communities as humble listeners who can learn culture, build trust, and serve without assuming they already understand.

Introductory course

This course is available as an initial public draft. It should be reviewed by responsible ministry leaders before use in formal training.

docs.title: Course Purpose
docs.text: This course introduces cultural humility, local respect, and practical listening habits for missionary service.

docs.title: Intended Audience
docs.text:
• Prospective cross-cultural workers.
  • Church members and families preparing for service.
  • Sponsors and small organizations supporting workers.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text: Recommended: Missionary Readiness 101.

docs.title: Learning Objectives
docs.text:
By the end of this course, learners should be able to:

  • Explain the difference between cultural awareness and cultural humility.
  • Practice observation and listening before offering solutions.
  • Identify assumptions that can harm cross-cultural relationships.
  • Prepare questions that support learning from local believers and community members.

docs.title: Estimated Time
docs.text: 60-75 minutes.

docs.title: Lessons
docs.text:
1. Entering As A Learner
  2. Knowledge Check

docs.title: Required Reading/Viewing
docs.text:
• Read the course overview.
  • Complete Lesson 01.
  • Review one reputable resource on cross-cultural listening or local leadership.

docs.title: Reflection Questions
docs.text:
1. What assumptions might you bring into a new culture?
  2. Who are the local leaders you would need to learn from?
  3. What would it look like to slow down before trying to help?

docs.title: Practical Assignment
docs.text: Interview someone with meaningful cross-cultural experience. Ask what helped them learn well and what mistakes they would caution against.

docs.title: Completion Criteria
docs.text:
• Review the overview and Lesson 01.
  • Answer the reflection questions.
  • Complete the knowledge check.
  • Complete the interview assignment and summarize what you learned.

docs.title: Certificate Of Completion Note
docs.text: This course may later be eligible for a certificate of completion. A certificate of completion is not an accredited certification, academic credit, ordination, commissioning, or professional license.

docs.title: Facilitator Notes
docs.text:
• Keep learners from treating culture as a problem to solve.
  • Emphasize local churches, local leaders, and patient learning.
  • Address superiority, savior language, and careless assumptions directly but constructively.

docs.title: Revision History
docs.text: Date Version Notes June 7, 2026 0.1 Initial public draft.

docs.title: Structure
docs.text:
• Question count: 5
  • Passing threshold: 4 correct responses
  • Question types: multiple choice and short answer
  • Retake guidance: Review Lesson 01 before trying again.

docs.title: 1. Multiple Choice
docs.text:
What should a learner do before offering solutions in a new culture?

  • A. Listen, observe, and ask careful questions.
  • B. Assume their home culture has the best answer.
  • C. Avoid local leaders.
  • D. Move quickly to prove usefulness.

Correct answer: A

docs.title: 2. Multiple Choice
docs.text:
Why should workers honor local leaders?

  • A. Local leaders understand context and carry responsibility.
  • B. Local leaders make preparation unnecessary.
  • C. Outsiders cannot learn anything.
  • D. It guarantees quick success.

Correct answer: A

docs.title: 3. Short Answer
docs.text:
Name one assumption a cross-cultural worker may need to examine.

Model answer: Assumptions about time, authority, family, money, communication, worship, gender roles, hospitality, or leadership.

docs.title: 4. Multiple Choice
docs.text:
What is one sign of cultural humility?

  • A. Correcting people before understanding them.
  • B. Receiving correction and learning patiently.
  • C. Avoiding all local relationships.
  • D. Treating culture as irrelevant.

Correct answer: B

docs.title: 5. Short Answer
docs.text:
What question could you ask a local believer or leader to learn well?

Model answer: A learner might ask, "What do outsiders often misunderstand here?" or "What should I learn before trying to help?"

docs.title: Completion Note
docs.text: This knowledge check supports reflection. It does not certify, commission, or approve anyone for missionary service.

docs.title: Lesson 01: Entering As A Learner
docs.text: Cross-cultural service begins with humility. A learner enters a new setting with respect, patience, and a willingness to be corrected.

docs.title: Key Idea
docs.text: Before serving well, learners must first learn to observe, listen, ask, and receive local wisdom.

docs.title: Listen Before Explaining
docs.text: New workers should resist quick conclusions. Listening helps reveal what people value, fear, celebrate, and need.

docs.title: Honor Local Leaders
docs.text: Local churches and leaders understand context in ways outsiders usually do not. Responsible workers seek their counsel and avoid bypassing them.

docs.title: Notice Assumptions
docs.text: Every learner carries assumptions about time, authority, family, money, communication, worship, and leadership. Naming those assumptions helps prevent harm.

docs.title: Serve With Patience
docs.text: Trust grows slowly. Faithful service often begins with small acts of reliability rather than visible leadership.

docs.title: Reflection
docs.text:
1. What do you tend to assume too quickly?
  2. What questions would help you learn from local leaders?
  3. How can you practice patient listening now?

docs.title: Lesson Summary
docs.text: Cross-cultural service requires a learner's posture. Humility is not weakness; it is part of faithful preparation.

docs.title: Field Readiness
docs.text:
Field Readiness helps learners prepare for practical field entry with clear expectations, wise planning, and healthy communication.

Introductory course

This course is available as an initial public draft. It should be reviewed by responsible ministry leaders before use in formal training.

docs.title: Course Purpose
docs.text: This course introduces practical questions learners should address before field involvement.

docs.title: Intended Audience
docs.text:
• Prospective missionaries and families.
  • Local churches and sponsors preparing workers.
  • Small organizations building basic field-entry expectations.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text: Recommended: Missionary Readiness 101, Missionary Care, and any required local or organizational training.

docs.title: Learning Objectives
docs.text:
By the end of this course, learners should be able to:

  • Identify practical questions that should be addressed before departure.
  • Prepare communication rhythms with sending partners.
  • Recognize basic risk, logistics, and transition concerns.
  • Clarify next steps with church, agency, or team leaders.

docs.title: Estimated Time
docs.text: 60-75 minutes.

docs.title: Lessons
docs.text:
1. Preparing For Field Entry
  2. Knowledge Check

docs.title: Required Reading/Viewing
docs.text:
• Read the course overview.
  • Complete Lesson 01.
  • Review field-specific requirements from your church, sponsor, organization, or local leaders.

docs.title: Reflection Questions
docs.text:
1. What practical uncertainty could create avoidable stress?
  2. What information do your sending partners need from you?
  3. Which field-specific questions require qualified guidance?

docs.title: Practical Assignment
docs.text: Create a field-readiness checklist covering documents, communication, support, health, risk, language learning, local expectations, and first-year priorities.

docs.title: Completion Criteria
docs.text:
• Review the overview and Lesson 01.
  • Answer the reflection questions.
  • Complete the knowledge check.
  • Draft and review a field-readiness checklist with responsible leaders.

docs.title: Certificate Of Completion Note
docs.text: This course may later be eligible for a certificate of completion. A certificate of completion is not an accredited certification, academic credit, ordination, commissioning, or professional license.

docs.title: Facilitator Notes
docs.text:
• Do not give legal, immigration, medical, tax, or security advice unless qualified.
  • Encourage learners to identify the appropriate responsible person for each field-specific question.
  • Keep safety, family needs, and local accountability visible.

Caution

This course does not provide legal, immigration, security, tax, or medical advice. Consult qualified professionals and responsible leaders for field-specific decisions.

docs.title: Revision History
docs.text: Date Version Notes June 7, 2026 0.1 Initial public draft.

docs.title: Structure
docs.text:
• Question count: 5
  • Passing threshold: 4 correct responses
  • Question types: multiple choice and short answer
  • Retake guidance: Review Lesson 01 before trying again.

docs.title: 1. Multiple Choice
docs.text:
What is one purpose of field readiness?

  • A. To clarify practical responsibilities before departure.
  • B. To avoid all uncertainty.
  • C. To replace local legal requirements.
  • D. To guarantee ministry results.

Correct answer: A

docs.title: 2. Multiple Choice
docs.text:
Which topic may require qualified professional guidance?

  • A. Favorite notebook style.
  • B. Legal, immigration, medical, tax, or security questions.
  • C. Preferred reading time.
  • D. Personal color preferences.

Correct answer: B

docs.title: 3. Short Answer
docs.text:
Name two items that could belong on a field-readiness checklist.

Model answer: Documents, visa questions, emergency contacts, insurance, communication rhythms, support plan, health preparation, risk awareness, local expectations, or first-year goals.

docs.title: 4. Multiple Choice
docs.text:
What should the first year often prioritize?

  • A. Public visibility above learning.
  • B. Language learning, relationships, local orientation, and realistic limits.
  • C. Avoiding local leaders.
  • D. Working without accountability.

Correct answer: B

docs.title: 5. Short Answer
docs.text:
Who could help answer field-specific questions responsibly?

Model answer: A sending organization, local church leader, field leader, qualified legal professional, medical professional, security advisor, or experienced mentor.

docs.title: Completion Note
docs.text: This knowledge check supports reflection. It does not provide legal, immigration, medical, tax, or security advice.

docs.title: Lesson 01: Preparing For Field Entry
docs.text: Field readiness brings practical questions into the open before avoidable stress becomes a crisis.

docs.title: Key Idea
docs.text: Good preparation helps learners clarify responsibilities, communicate expectations, and seek qualified guidance where needed.

docs.title: Documents And Requirements
docs.text: Workers may need passports, visas, permissions, insurance, emergency contacts, training records, or organization-specific documents.

docs.title: Communication
docs.text: Churches, sponsors, family, and team leaders need clear rhythms for updates, urgent concerns, and confidential matters.

docs.title: Health And Safety
docs.text: Health preparation, child protection, risk awareness, and emergency planning should be addressed with qualified leaders and professionals.

docs.title: First-Year Priorities
docs.text: The first year often requires language learning, relationship building, local orientation, and realistic limits.

docs.title: Reflection
docs.text:
1. What field-entry topic do you understand least?
  2. Who can answer field-specific questions responsibly?
  3. What communication rhythm would help your supporters?

docs.title: Lesson Summary
docs.text: Field readiness is not about controlling every outcome. It is about preparing responsibly and knowing where wisdom, authority, and qualified help are needed.

docs.title: Language Learning
docs.text:
Language Learning introduces realistic expectations and practical habits for learners preparing to communicate in a new context.

Introductory course

This course is available as an initial public draft. It should be reviewed by responsible ministry leaders before use in formal training.

docs.title: Course Purpose
docs.text: This course helps learners treat language learning as an act of love, humility, and practical preparation.

docs.title: Intended Audience
docs.text:
• Prospective missionaries.
  • Mission-minded families.
  • Churches and sponsors supporting language learners.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text: Recommended: Missionary Readiness 101 and Cross-Cultural Service.

docs.title: Learning Objectives
docs.text:
By the end of this course, learners should be able to:

  • Set realistic expectations for language acquisition.
  • Describe daily habits that support progress.
  • Practice humility when communication is slow or uncomfortable.
  • Plan for language learning as ministry preparation, not a side task.

docs.title: Estimated Time
docs.text: 45-60 minutes.

docs.title: Lessons
docs.text:
1. Language Learning As Love
  2. Knowledge Check

docs.title: Required Reading/Viewing
docs.text:
• Read the course overview.
  • Complete Lesson 01.
  • Identify one language-learning resource or local helper option for your context.

docs.title: Reflection Questions
docs.text:
1. How might language learning reshape your posture toward the people you hope to serve?
  2. What frustrations do you expect?
  3. What daily habit could you begin now?

docs.title: Practical Assignment
docs.text: Create a two-week language-learning practice plan with daily listening, review, and conversation goals.

docs.title: Completion Criteria
docs.text:
• Review the overview and Lesson 01.
  • Answer the reflection questions.
  • Complete the knowledge check.
  • Draft a two-week practice plan.

docs.title: Certificate Of Completion Note
docs.text: This course may later be eligible for a certificate of completion. A certificate of completion is not an accredited certification, academic credit, ordination, commissioning, or professional license.

docs.title: Facilitator Notes
docs.text:
• Encourage patience and realistic expectations.
  • Avoid shaming learners for slow progress.
  • Emphasize listening, local helpers, and consistency over shortcuts.

docs.title: Revision History
docs.text: Date Version Notes June 7, 2026 0.1 Initial public draft.

docs.title: Structure
docs.text:
• Question count: 5
  • Passing threshold: 4 correct responses
  • Question types: multiple choice and short answer
  • Retake guidance: Review Lesson 01 before trying again.

docs.title: 1. Multiple Choice
docs.text:
Why does language learning matter for missionary readiness?

  • A. It helps workers honor people and communicate with humility.
  • B. It guarantees fast ministry growth.
  • C. It removes the need for local relationships.
  • D. It is only useful for academic learners.

Correct answer: A

docs.title: 2. Multiple Choice
docs.text:
What should learners expect?

  • A. Immediate fluency.
  • B. No embarrassment.
  • C. Slow progress, mistakes, and repeated practice.
  • D. No need for local help.

Correct answer: C

docs.title: 3. Short Answer
docs.text:
Name one possible local helper for language learning.

Model answer: Tutor, neighbor, church member, conversation partner, teacher, or local friend.

docs.title: 4. Multiple Choice
docs.text:
What usually supports progress best?

  • A. Small daily habits.
  • B. Waiting until perfect motivation.
  • C. Avoiding conversation.
  • D. Only reading about the language.

Correct answer: A

docs.title: 5. Short Answer
docs.text:
What is one language-learning habit you could practice for two weeks?

Model answer: Daily listening, vocabulary review, pronunciation practice, short conversations, or meeting with a helper.

docs.title: Completion Note
docs.text: This knowledge check supports reflection. It does not certify language proficiency.

docs.title: Lesson 01: Language Learning As Love
docs.text: Language learning is more than a practical skill. It is a way to honor people, listen carefully, and enter community with humility.

docs.title: Key Idea
docs.text: Learning a language is a long act of respect. It helps workers serve as guests and learners rather than as distant outsiders.

docs.title: Set Realistic Expectations
docs.text: Language learning takes time. Learners should expect embarrassment, fatigue, repeated mistakes, and slow progress.

docs.title: Listen First
docs.text: Listening builds pronunciation, rhythm, vocabulary, and cultural understanding. Early listening is not wasted time.

docs.title: Use Local Helpers
docs.text: Tutors, neighbors, church members, and conversation partners can help learners practice in real settings.

docs.title: Practice Daily
docs.text: Small daily habits usually help more than occasional intense effort.

docs.title: Reflection
docs.text:
1. What makes language learning intimidating?
  2. Who could help you practice?
  3. What daily rhythm is realistic this month?

docs.title: Lesson Summary
docs.text: Language learning is part of faithful presence. It helps workers listen, respect, and serve more personally.

docs.title: Missionary Care
docs.text:
Missionary Care helps learners and sending teams pursue healthy, sustainable service through wise support, communication, boundaries, and care planning.

Introductory course

This course is available as an initial public draft. It should be reviewed by responsible ministry leaders before use in formal training.

docs.title: Course Purpose
docs.text: This course introduces whole-person care and support planning for missionary life.

docs.title: Intended Audience
docs.text:
• Prospective missionaries and families.
  • Churches, sponsors, mentors, and care teams.
  • Small organizations preparing or supporting workers.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text: Recommended: Missionary Readiness 101.

docs.title: Learning Objectives
docs.text:
By the end of this course, learners should be able to:

  • Name common stressors in missionary life.
  • Build a basic care plan with spiritual, relational, emotional, and practical supports.
  • Communicate needs clearly with churches, mentors, and team leaders.
  • Recognize when professional or pastoral help is needed.

docs.title: Estimated Time
docs.text: 60-75 minutes.

docs.title: Lessons
docs.text:
1. Care Is Part Of Readiness
  2. Knowledge Check

docs.title: Required Reading/Viewing
docs.text:
• Read the course overview.
  • Complete Lesson 01.
  • Review any care expectations from your church, sponsor, or sending organization.

docs.title: Reflection Questions
docs.text:
1. Who currently knows the honest state of your readiness?
  2. What support do you need before service begins?
  3. What warning signs would mean you need help?

docs.title: Practical Assignment
docs.text: Draft a basic care plan with names, communication rhythms, escalation steps, and support needs.

docs.title: Completion Criteria
docs.text:
• Review the overview and Lesson 01.
  • Answer the reflection questions.
  • Complete the knowledge check.
  • Draft a care plan for discussion with trusted leaders.

docs.title: Certificate Of Completion Note
docs.text: This course may later be eligible for a certificate of completion. A certificate of completion is not an accredited certification, academic credit, ordination, commissioning, or professional license.

docs.title: Facilitator Notes
docs.text:
• Do not pressure learners to share private details in a group setting.
  • Encourage clear boundaries and appropriate referral.
  • Treat family safety, child protection, crisis care, and counseling needs with seriousness.

Note

This course is educational and does not replace pastoral care, counseling, medical care, or crisis support.

docs.title: Revision History
docs.text: Date Version Notes June 7, 2026 0.1 Initial public draft.

docs.title: Structure
docs.text:
• Question count: 5
  • Passing threshold: 4 correct responses
  • Question types: multiple choice and short answer
  • Retake guidance: Review Lesson 01 before trying again.

docs.title: 1. Multiple Choice
docs.text:
When should care planning begin?

  • A. Only after a crisis.
  • B. Before, during, and after field involvement.
  • C. Only after language learning.
  • D. Only if someone fails.

Correct answer: B

docs.title: 2. Multiple Choice
docs.text:
Which issue may require qualified professional or official help?

  • A. A typo in a newsletter.
  • B. A reading schedule preference.
  • C. A child protection, medical, counseling, legal, or security concern.
  • D. A favorite worship song.

Correct answer: C

docs.title: 3. Short Answer
docs.text:
Name two possible missionary stressors.

Model answer: Culture shock, language fatigue, family strain, team conflict, financial pressure, loneliness, disappointment, or unclear expectations.

docs.title: 4. Multiple Choice
docs.text:
Why are communication rhythms useful?

  • A. They help supporters respond to real needs.
  • B. They replace personal responsibility.
  • C. They guarantee no conflict.
  • D. They make privacy unnecessary.

Correct answer: A

docs.title: 5. Short Answer
docs.text:
Who might belong on a basic care plan?

Model answer: Pastor, mentor, sponsor, family contact, medical provider, counselor, field leader, or organization contact.

docs.title: Completion Note
docs.text: This knowledge check supports reflection. It does not replace pastoral care, counseling, medical care, or crisis support.

docs.title: Lesson 01: Care Is Part Of Readiness
docs.text: Missionary care is not a sign of weakness. It is part of responsible preparation for service that can involve stress, transition, conflict, loneliness, fatigue, or uncertainty.

docs.title: Key Idea
docs.text: Healthy service requires support before, during, and after field involvement.

docs.title: Name Likely Stressors
docs.text: Common stressors include culture shock, language fatigue, family strain, team conflict, financial pressure, loneliness, disappointment, and unclear expectations.

docs.title: Build Support Early
docs.text: Care is stronger when relationships already exist. Learners should identify pastors, mentors, sponsors, medical providers, counselors, and field contacts before a crisis.

docs.title: Communicate Clearly
docs.text: Supporters cannot respond well to vague or hidden needs. Learners should prepare honest communication rhythms that respect privacy and safety.

docs.title: Know When To Escalate
docs.text: Some issues require pastoral care, professional counseling, medical attention, child protection reporting, legal help, or security guidance.

docs.title: Reflection
docs.text:
1. What stressors are most likely for you or your family?
  2. Who should be on your care list?
  3. What would make it hard for you to ask for help?

docs.title: Lesson Summary
docs.text: Missionary care belongs at the beginning of readiness planning, not only after something goes wrong.

docs.title: Missionary Readiness 101
docs.text:
Missionary Readiness 101 is a model starter course for the platform. It introduces the basic idea that missionary service requires more than willingness. Faithful readiness includes spiritual formation, humility, accountability, safety, cultural respect, and practical preparation.

Introductory course

This course is available as an initial public draft. It should be reviewed by responsible ministry leaders before use in formal training.

docs.title: Course Purpose
docs.text: This course helps learners understand the core areas of missionary readiness and begin an honest preparation conversation with a church leader, mentor, sponsor, or sending partner.

docs.title: Intended Audience
docs.text:
• Independent missionaries exploring next steps.
  • Church members discerning missionary involvement.
  • Mission-minded families preparing for service.
  • Local churches and sponsors supporting workers.
  • Small organizations building basic readiness expectations.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text: None. This course is designed as an entry point.

docs.title: Learning Objectives
docs.text:
By the end of this course, learners should be able to:

  • Explain why readiness includes spiritual, relational, cultural, safety, and practical concerns.
  • Identify personal strengths and preparation gaps.
  • Describe why local churches, local leaders, and accountable relationships matter.
  • Name next steps for continued preparation.

docs.title: Estimated Time
docs.text: 45-60 minutes.

docs.title: Lessons
docs.text:
1. What Missionary Readiness Means
  2. Knowledge Check

docs.title: Required Reading/Viewing
docs.text:
• Read this course overview.
  • Complete Lesson 01.
  • Review the platform Mission & Vision.

docs.title: Reflection Questions
docs.text:
1. What attracts you to missionary service?
  2. What parts of readiness feel most familiar?
  3. What parts of readiness do you most need to discuss with a trusted leader?

docs.title: Practical Assignment
docs.text: Write a one-page readiness reflection. Include your current sense of calling, support relationships, cross-cultural experience, safety concerns, and three next steps for preparation.

docs.title: Completion Criteria
docs.text:
Learners complete this course by:

  • Reading the course overview and Lesson 01.
  • Answering the reflection questions.
  • Completing the knowledge check.
  • Drafting the practical assignment for discussion with a mentor, pastor, sponsor, or sending partner.

docs.title: Certificate Of Completion Note
docs.text: This model course may later be eligible for a certificate of completion if the platform supports that feature. A certificate of completion is not an accredited certification, academic credit, ordination, commissioning, or professional license.

docs.title: Facilitator Notes
docs.text:
• Use this course as a conversation starter, not as a final readiness assessment.
  • Encourage honest reflection rather than polished answers.
  • If learners raise legal, medical, counseling, child protection, tax, immigration, or security concerns, refer them to qualified leaders or professionals.

docs.title: Revision History
docs.text: Date Version Notes June 7, 2026 0.1 Initial public draft.

docs.title: Knowledge Check
docs.text: This knowledge check is formative. It is meant to help learners confirm basic understanding before discussing the course with a mentor, pastor, sponsor, or sending partner.

docs.title: Structure
docs.text:
• Question count: 5
  • Passing threshold: 4 correct responses
  • Question types: multiple choice and short answer
  • Retake guidance: Review Lesson 01 before trying again.

docs.title: 1. Multiple Choice
docs.text:
Which statement best describes missionary readiness?

  • A. A guarantee that someone is prepared for every field situation.
  • B. A process of preparing to serve faithfully, safely, respectfully, and accountably.
  • C. A replacement for local church discernment.
  • D. An accredited credential for missionary service.

Correct answer: B

docs.title: 2. Multiple Choice
docs.text:
Why does cross-cultural humility matter?

  • A. It helps workers enter communities as learners.
  • B. It removes the need for local leaders.
  • C. It guarantees quick ministry success.
  • D. It replaces language learning.

Correct answer: A

docs.title: 3. Short Answer
docs.text:
Name two people or groups who should be part of a healthy readiness conversation.

Model answer: A learner might name a pastor, mentor, local church, family member, sponsor, sending organization, field leader, or trusted ministry advisor.

docs.title: 4. Multiple Choice
docs.text:
Which issue should not be handled through a general public contact form?

  • A. A typo on a public page.
  • B. A question about course navigation.
  • C. A sensitive legal, medical, counseling, child protection, immigration, tax, or security concern.
  • D. A suggestion for clearer wording.

Correct answer: C

docs.title: 5. Short Answer
docs.text:
What is one practical next step a learner can take after this course?

Model answer: A learner might write a readiness reflection, meet with a pastor or mentor, review safety concerns, identify preparation gaps, or create a next-step plan.

docs.title: Completion Note
docs.text: Record your answers and discuss any missed questions with a trusted leader or facilitator. This knowledge check does not certify, commission, or approve anyone for missionary service.

docs.title: Lesson 01: What Missionary Readiness Means
docs.text: Missionary readiness is the sober preparation needed before serving across cultures, communities, or ministry contexts. It is not only a matter of desire. It includes formation, wisdom, support, humility, and practical responsibility.

docs.title: Key Idea
docs.text: Readiness is the process of becoming prepared to serve faithfully, safely, respectfully, and accountably.

docs.title: Spiritual Formation
docs.text: Missionary service should grow from worship, Scripture, prayer, obedience, and character. Skills matter, but character carries weight when plans change, language is difficult, relationships are strained, or ministry fruit is slow.

docs.title: Cross-Cultural Humility
docs.text: Missionaries enter communities as learners. Cultural respect includes listening carefully, honoring local leaders, avoiding superiority, and taking time to understand the people and place before acting.

docs.title: Accountability And Support
docs.text: Independent service should not mean isolated service. Healthy preparation includes trusted pastors, mentors, churches, sponsors, family conversations, and field relationships that can ask hard questions and provide care.

docs.title: Safety And Ethical Practice
docs.text: Readiness includes attention to family safety, child protection, legal compliance, appropriate boundaries, and responsible communication. Some concerns require qualified professional guidance and should not be handled casually.

docs.title: Practical Preparation
docs.text: Faithful service also requires ordinary planning: documents, finances, communication rhythms, language learning, health preparation, local expectations, and clear next steps.

docs.title: Reflection
docs.text:
Before moving on, write brief answers to these questions:

  1. Which area of readiness is strongest for you right now?
  2. Which area needs the most attention?
  3. Who should be part of your readiness conversation?

docs.title: Lesson Summary
docs.text: Missionary readiness is not a badge or status. It is a posture and preparation process that helps workers serve with humility, faithfulness, accountability, and practical wisdom.

docs.title: Spiritual Formation
docs.text:
Spiritual Formation prepares learners for missionary service rooted in worship, Scripture, prayer, obedience, and durable love for God and neighbor.

Introductory course

This course is available as an initial public draft. It should be reviewed by responsible ministry leaders before use in formal training.

docs.title: Course Purpose
docs.text: This course helps learners examine the spiritual practices, character concerns, and accountability relationships needed for faithful missionary service.

docs.title: Intended Audience
docs.text:
• Prospective missionaries.
  • Church members preparing for cross-cultural service.
  • Mentors, pastors, and sponsors discussing readiness.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text: Recommended: Missionary Readiness 101.

docs.title: Learning Objectives
docs.text:
By the end of this course, learners should be able to:

  • Describe why spiritual formation is central to readiness.
  • Identify rhythms that sustain prayer, Scripture, worship, and rest.
  • Recognize pressures that can expose unhealthy expectations.
  • Discuss spiritual readiness honestly with trusted leaders.

docs.title: Estimated Time
docs.text: 60-75 minutes.

docs.title: Lessons
docs.text:
1. Formation Before Activity
  2. Knowledge Check

docs.title: Required Reading/Viewing
docs.text:
• Read the course overview.
  • Complete Lesson 01.
  • Read a selected Scripture passage on character, prayer, or endurance.

docs.title: Reflection Questions
docs.text:
1. Where are you tempted to measure readiness by activity rather than faithfulness?
  2. Which spiritual rhythm most needs attention before service?
  3. Who can ask you honest questions about your character and practices?

docs.title: Practical Assignment
docs.text: Draft a simple weekly rule of life for the next month. Include Scripture, prayer, worship, rest, service, and one accountability conversation.

docs.title: Completion Criteria
docs.text:
• Review the overview and Lesson 01.
  • Answer the reflection questions.
  • Complete the knowledge check.
  • Draft and discuss the practical assignment with a mentor, pastor, or trusted leader.

docs.title: Certificate Of Completion Note
docs.text: This course may later be eligible for a certificate of completion. A certificate of completion is not an accredited certification, academic credit, ordination, commissioning, or professional license.

docs.title: Facilitator Notes
docs.text:
• Keep the discussion concrete and personal without forcing disclosure.
  • Watch for signs that a learner needs pastoral care or professional support.
  • Encourage durable practices rather than short-term intensity.

docs.title: Revision History
docs.text: Date Version Notes June 7, 2026 0.1 Initial public draft.

docs.title: Structure
docs.text:
• Question count: 5
  • Passing threshold: 4 correct responses
  • Question types: multiple choice and short answer
  • Retake guidance: Review Lesson 01 before trying again.

docs.title: 1. Multiple Choice
docs.text:
What best describes spiritual formation?

  • A. A way to look impressive in ministry.
  • B. The ongoing shaping of character, worship, prayer, obedience, and community.
  • C. A replacement for practical preparation.
  • D. A guarantee that missionary service will be easy.

Correct answer: B

docs.title: 2. Multiple Choice
docs.text:
Why does rest matter for readiness?

  • A. It helps learners recognize limits and resist unhealthy productivity.
  • B. It removes the need for planning.
  • C. It proves someone is spiritually mature.
  • D. It replaces accountability.

Correct answer: A

docs.title: 3. Short Answer
docs.text:
Name two spiritual practices that can support durable service.

Model answer: Scripture, prayer, worship, obedience, repentance, rest, accountability, or local church participation.

docs.title: 4. Multiple Choice
docs.text:
What should public ministry avoid doing?

  • A. Growing from private faithfulness.
  • B. Serving local communities.
  • C. Outrunning private faithfulness and character.
  • D. Involving trusted leaders.

Correct answer: C

docs.title: 5. Short Answer
docs.text:
Who could help you discuss spiritual readiness honestly?

Model answer: A pastor, mentor, elder, trusted friend, sponsor, sending leader, or mature believer.

docs.title: Completion Note
docs.text: This knowledge check supports reflection. It does not certify, commission, or approve anyone for missionary service.

docs.title: Lesson 01: Formation Before Activity
docs.text: Missionary service can expose the difference between visible activity and quiet faithfulness. Spiritual formation is the ongoing work of being shaped by God through Scripture, prayer, worship, obedience, repentance, rest, and community.

docs.title: Key Idea
docs.text: Missionary readiness begins with the kind of person a learner is becoming, not only with the tasks a learner hopes to perform.

docs.title: Scripture And Prayer
docs.text: Workers need regular habits of listening to God before speaking for God. Scripture and prayer provide stability when culture, language, relationships, or plans feel unfamiliar.

docs.title: Worship And Obedience
docs.text: Readiness includes love for God expressed in ordinary obedience. Public ministry should not outrun private faithfulness.

docs.title: Rest And Limits
docs.text: Mission work can reward urgency. Learners should prepare to receive limits, practice rest, and resist measuring worth by productivity alone.

docs.title: Accountability
docs.text: Trusted leaders and peers can help learners notice blind spots, confess honestly, and stay teachable.

docs.title: Reflection
docs.text:
1. Which practice is currently strongest?
  2. Which practice is most neglected?
  3. What would a sustainable next step look like this week?

docs.title: Lesson Summary
docs.text: Spiritual formation is not a decorative add-on to missionary service. It is part of the foundation for faithful, humble, and durable ministry.

docs.title: Policies
docs.text:
This section gathers public policy and compliance pages for the missionary readiness platform.

Starter template

These policy pages are starter templates for a small nonprofit-style educational project. They should be reviewed before formal launch and before relying on them publicly.

The site is currently static and hosted on GitHub Pages. It does not currently collect user accounts directly, process payments directly, or claim accreditation. External tools such as Google Forms, email, GitHub Pages, or analytics may be used later and should be disclosed when added.

docs.title: Current Pages
docs.text:
• Privacy Policy
  • Terms of Use
  • Acceptable Use
  • Copyright & License
  • Disclaimer

docs.title: Review Priorities
docs.text:
• Keep contact information and effective dates current.
  • Confirm the responsible legal or ministry organization.
  • Confirm the final content and code license.
  • Avoid unsupported claims about accreditation, legal compliance, safety procedures, or professional services.
  • Update policies before collecting personal information, using external forms, enabling analytics, accepting payments, or issuing certificates of completion.

docs.title: Acceptable Use
docs.text:
Effective date: June 7, 2026

Starter template

This acceptable use policy is starter text for a small nonprofit-style educational project. It should be reviewed and completed before formal launch.

This site exists to support practical missionary readiness training. Visitors are expected to use the site in a lawful, respectful, and responsible way.

docs.title: Appropriate Use
docs.text:
You may use this site to:

  • Read and share public training content.
  • Adapt eligible materials according to the stated license.
  • Contact the project with questions, corrections, or partnership inquiries.
  • Use course content for personal, church, family, sponsor, or small-organization preparation.

docs.title: Prohibited Use
docs.text:
Do not use this site to:

  • Harass, threaten, exploit, or harm others.
  • Submit unlawful, abusive, misleading, or malicious content.
  • Attempt to disrupt, scan, attack, or compromise the site or its hosting providers.
  • Misrepresent the platform as an accredited institution or degree-granting program.
  • Claim that course participation certifies, appoints, deploys, or professionally qualifies someone for missionary service.
  • Remove attribution, imply endorsement, or present site content as your own.
  • Use the content in ways that disrespect local churches, local leaders, family safety, child protection, legal compliance, or ethical mission practice.

docs.title: Sensitive Information
docs.text:
Do not submit sensitive personal information through general contact forms, email links, public issue trackers, or other general communication channels connected to this site.

Sensitive information includes medical, counseling, legal, immigration, tax, financial, child protection, field security, crisis, or confidential pastoral details.

docs.title: External Tools
docs.text: The site may later link to external tools such as Google Forms, email services, analytics, or course platforms. Use those tools according to their own terms and privacy policies.

docs.title: Reporting Concerns
docs.text:
Concerns about misuse, errors, or unsafe content may be sent to robert@tetra6.com.

For urgent safety, legal, medical, child protection, or security matters, contact the appropriate local authority, qualified professional, church leader, or organization-specific reporting process.

docs.title: Copyright & License
docs.text:
Effective date: June 7, 2026

Starter template

This copyright and license page is starter text for a small nonprofit-style educational project. It should be reviewed and completed before formal launch.

Unless otherwise marked, public training content on this site is made available under the Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International license.

docs.title: What This Means
docs.text: Under the Creative Commons Attribution license, you may generally share and adapt eligible content, including for church, family, sponsor, small-organization, or educational use, as long as you provide appropriate attribution and do not imply endorsement.

docs.title: Attribution
docs.text:
When reusing or adapting material, use attribution like:

Adapted from Missionary Readiness Institute, https://missionary-readiness-institute.github.io/readiness-platform/

If you make changes, note that your version has been adapted.

docs.title: What Is Not Covered
docs.text:
This license does not automatically apply to:

  • Logos, trademarks, branding, or organization names.
  • Third-party content, quotations, images, or resources that have their own license terms.
  • Private training materials not published on this site.
  • Contributor content governed by a separate agreement.
  • Certificates of completion, learner records, or administrative documents if those features are added later.
  • Site code, workflows, or configuration unless a software license is added separately.

docs.title: No Endorsement
docs.text: Reuse of content does not mean Missionary Readiness Institute endorses a person, church, organization, adaptation, translation, training program, or sending decision.

docs.title: Questions
docs.text: Questions about reuse or attribution may be sent to robert@tetra6.com.

docs.title: Disclaimer
docs.text:
Effective date: June 7, 2026

Starter template

This disclaimer is starter text for a small nonprofit-style educational project. It should be reviewed and completed before formal launch.

The training content on this site is educational. It is intended to support thoughtful missionary readiness conversations, not replace qualified professional advice or responsible leadership.

docs.title: Not Professional Advice
docs.text: The content does not replace legal, medical, security, immigration, tax, counseling, financial, child protection, or other professional advice. Consult qualified professionals and responsible leaders for decisions in those areas.

docs.title: Not Accreditation
docs.text: Missionary Readiness Institute is not currently an accredited institution, university, seminary, or degree-granting program. Course completion certificates may be offered later, but a certificate of completion is not the same as accredited certification, academic credit, ordination, commissioning, or professional licensure.

docs.title: Not Sending Approval
docs.text: Using this site does not approve, appoint, send, deploy, or endorse any person for missionary service. Readiness and sending decisions should remain with appropriate churches, sending organizations, families, sponsors, mentors, and field leaders.

docs.title: Field-Specific Responsibility
docs.text: Mission work varies widely by country, culture, legal setting, family situation, church relationship, and security environment. Users are responsible for seeking field-specific guidance from qualified local leaders, sending partners, legal authorities, and professionals where appropriate.

docs.title: External Links And Tools
docs.text: This site may link to external resources or tools. Links are provided for convenience and do not necessarily imply endorsement. External services are governed by their own policies and terms.

docs.title: Sensitive Information
docs.text: Do not submit sensitive personal information through general contact forms, email links, or other public channels connected to this site. Use appropriate confidential, professional, or official reporting channels instead.

docs.title: Privacy Policy
docs.text:
Effective date: June 7, 2026

Starter template

This privacy policy is starter text for a small nonprofit-style educational project. It should be reviewed and completed before formal launch.

Missionary Readiness Institute currently operates this site as a static GitHub Pages / MkDocs Material website. The site does not currently provide user accounts, directly collect course registrations, or directly process payments.

docs.title: Information We Collect
docs.text:
This site is primarily informational. We may receive limited information if visitors contact us directly, submit feedback through a linked external tool, or interact with third-party services used to host or measure the site.

Possible information may include:

  • Name, email address, and message content if a visitor provides it voluntarily.
  • Basic technical information handled by GitHub Pages or other hosting services, such as IP address, browser type, device information, and access logs.
  • Analytics information if analytics tools are added later.
  • Information submitted through external tools, such as Google Forms or email, if those tools are linked from the site.

docs.title: Sensitive Information
docs.text: Do not submit sensitive personal information through general contact forms, public links, or email addresses listed on this site. This includes medical, counseling, legal, immigration, tax, financial, child protection, security, or crisis information.

docs.title: How We Use Information
docs.text:
Information may be used to:

  • Respond to inquiries.
  • Improve site content and usability.
  • Maintain site security and reliability.
  • Communicate about training resources or partnerships when requested.

docs.title: What We Do Not Do
docs.text:
We do not intend to sell visitor personal information. We do not intend to collect sensitive pastoral, medical, legal, immigration, child protection, tax, counseling, financial, or security information through this public site.

We do not currently create user accounts directly on this site or handle payment processing directly through this site.

docs.title: Third-Party Services
docs.text:
The site is currently hosted through GitHub Pages. GitHub and other third-party providers may process technical data according to their own policies.

External tools may be used later for forms, email, analytics, embedded media, course communication, or certificates of completion. If those tools are added, visitors should review the privacy practices of those services, and this policy should be updated as needed.

docs.title: Data Retention
docs.text: Messages and technical records should be retained only as long as needed for ministry, operational, security, or legal purposes.

docs.title: Contact
docs.text: Questions about this policy may be sent to robert@tetra6.com.

docs.title: Terms of Use
docs.text:
Effective date: June 7, 2026

Starter template

These terms are starter text for a small nonprofit-style educational project. They should be reviewed and completed before formal launch.

docs.title: Use of This Site
docs.text: This site provides public educational and ministry training resources. By using the site, visitors agree to use the material responsibly, lawfully, and in a way that does not disrupt the site or misuse its content.

docs.title: Educational Purpose
docs.text:
The content is provided for general training and reflection. It is not a substitute for pastoral care, professional counseling, medical care, legal advice, immigration advice, security consulting, child protection reporting, or official sending-organization requirements.

See the Disclaimer for more detail.

docs.title: No Accreditation Or Degree
docs.text: This platform is not a university, seminary, accredited institution, or degree-granting program. It may later offer certificates of completion for course participation, but it does not currently provide accredited certification, academic credit, or professional licensure.

docs.title: No Sending Approval
docs.text: Using or completing content on this site does not commission, appoint, endorse, approve, or deploy any person for missionary service. Sending and readiness decisions remain with the appropriate churches, organizations, leaders, families, sponsors, and workers.

docs.title: Intellectual Property
docs.text: Unless otherwise stated, site content is made available under the terms described in Copyright & License. Visitors may not present the material as their own, imply endorsement, remove required attribution, or use the content in a misleading way.

docs.title: Acceptable Use
docs.text: Visitors should use the site respectfully and lawfully. Do not attempt to disrupt the site, misuse contact channels, submit sensitive personal information through general forms, or use the material to mislead others. See Acceptable Use.

docs.title: External Links
docs.text: This site may link to third-party resources. Those links are provided for convenience and do not necessarily imply endorsement. Third-party sites are governed by their own terms and policies.

docs.title: Payments And Accounts
docs.text: This static site does not currently create user accounts directly or process payments directly. If external registration, forms, payment tools, or course platforms are added later, those services may have their own terms and privacy policies.

docs.title: Changes
docs.text: These terms may be updated as the site and training platform develop. The effective date should be revised when material changes are made.

docs.title: Contact
docs.text: Questions about these terms may be sent to robert@tetra6.com.
